topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope sat on the chipped Formica countertop, a stark white declaration against the dull grey of the kitchen. Leo traced the embossed lettering with a trembling finger. Condo. The word echoed in the silence of his cramped studio, a hollow sound that seemed to vibrate in his chest. He stared at the peeling paint on the windowsill, the view of neighboring balconies suddenly seeming like a spotlight illuminating his own inadequacy. Every small imperfection in the room—the crooked picture frame, the stain on the carpet—felt magnified, accusations whispered by the impending change. He picked up his phone, the screen illuminating the pale sheen of sweat on his palms. He didn't call anyone; the silence felt safer, a temporary shield against the inevitable exposure of his own smallness.

[story 2]

"They're selling the whole complex," Mrs. Henderson announced, her voice brittle as old glass, over the lukewarm coffee in the lobby. Mark just nodded, his gaze fixed on the scuff marks on the polished marble floor. He felt a strange, heavy numbness settling over him, like deep-sea pressure. He watched the other residents—the ones who seemed to possess an inherent grace, who moved through their lives with an easy confidence—and felt a distinct separation, a chasm opening between him and them. When he finally looked at his own reflection in the darkened glass of the elevator doors, the face staring back seemed unfamiliar, a stranger whose features held no discernible appeal. He just wanted to disappear into the texture of the worn linoleum.

[story 3]

It wasn't the news itself that broke him; it was the realization that his meticulously curated, tiny existence was about to be erased. Sarah stood by the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. She didn't cry. Instead, a slow, deliberate movement took over her. She began methodically stacking the few books on the shelf, aligning the spines with obsessive precision. Each book placed felt like a tiny, futile act of control in a world that was rapidly dissolving around her. A deep, aching fatigue settled in her limbs, making her movements heavy and slow. She closed her eyes, trying to memorize the exact shade of the twilight filtering through the glass, a final, futile attempt to anchor herself to something real before the foundations shifted entirely.
